title: Parliament received a Bill to widen VAT, bring cross‑border digital services into scope, and raise the rate on financial services to 20.5%.
body_paragraphs: The draft law cut the standard registration threshold to Rs. 36 million a year from Rs. 60 million, pulling more small and medium enterprises into the net at a time when headline inflation had re‑accelerated above the Central Bank’s 5% midpoint target. It also proposed that non‑resident platforms supplying consumers in Sri Lanka register and account for tax, a change that would end the de facto zero‑rating of popular apps and streaming services and align the regime with peers that have already moved to destination‑based taxation of digital consumption.

The measure followed a sharp rise in consumer prices driven by higher energy costs linked to the Middle East conflict, with year‑on‑year inflation in Colombo hitting 5.4% in April after averaging about 2% in the prior quarter. While the Inland Revenue Department set a record Rs. 2,244 billion collection in 2025 and is tasked with Rs. 2,402 billion in 2026, the draft also tightened compliance: penalties were scaled up to Rs. 1 million and secured point‑of‑sale systems were mandated to close leakages that had widened during the crisis years.

By expanding the base while lifting the effective charge on banks and finance to 20.5% from 18%, the package shifted a larger burden onto sectors that had remained relatively profitable during the downturn. Zero‑rating for specific export‑linked service niches such as garment buying offices was retained, but the inclusion of digital platforms mirrored reforms across ASEAN and the OECD in recent years, with Sri Lanka moving from laggard to late‑mover status on this front.
bold_phrases: cut the standard registration threshold to Rs. 36 million
non‑resident platforms supplying consumers in Sri Lanka register
year‑on‑year inflation in Colombo hitting 5.4% in April
penalties were scaled up to Rs. 1 million

title: The Asian Development Bank sharply downgraded its growth outlook for developing Asia and Pacific to 4.7% for 2026.
body_paragraphs: The revision, from 5.1% in April’s baseline, came as the Middle East conflict pushed oil to an assumed $96 a barrel for the year, up from a pre‑war average of $69 in January–February and a level that would strain fuel‑importing economies such as Sri Lanka where energy makes up a significant share of imports. Inflation in the region was projected at 5.2% this year versus 3% in 2025, with an upside risk scenario of 7.4% if disruptions deepen, underscoring why Colombo’s consumer prices also accelerated after three consecutive months near 2%.

ADB urged Governments to let some price signals pass through while cushioning the most vulnerable with time‑bound, targeted support, warning that blanket subsidies would misallocate resources and worsen fiscal pressures just as debt‑service costs were rising. It also advised central banks to contain volatility and anchor expectations rather than overtighten into a negative supply shock that could choke fragile recoveries.

For Sri Lanka, the guidance aligned with the IMF programme’s architecture: revenue‑based consolidation alongside tighter monetary policy in 2023–24 had restored disinflation, but the second‑round effects of imported energy would test policy discipline during 2026 even as growth depends on tourism, remittances and a reopening of private credit.
bold_phrases: Inflation in the region was projected at 5.2%

title: Sri Lanka and the Maldives signed seven cooperation agreements during a two‑day State visit to Colombo by President Mohamed Muizzu.
body_paragraphs: The pacts covered tourism, defence education, counter‑terrorism, youth and sports, teacher training, and archival cooperation, expanding an already dense network of people‑to‑people and commercial links that made the Maldives Sri Lanka’s third‑largest SAARC export market in 2025 by value. The leaders also announced reciprocal land allocations for their High Commissions in Colombo and Malé, a long‑term institutional signal beyond the customary communiqués.

A business forum scheduled alongside the talks targeted opportunities in fisheries processing, boat‑building and logistics, with Sri Lanka inviting Maldivian investment in information technology, artificial intelligence (AI), real estate and leisure. With bilateral non‑oil trade between the Maldives and Sri Lanka estimated at under $1 billion in 2025, even modest project execution under the memoranda would move the needle relative to the small base, particularly given Malé’s interest in Bank of Maldives establishing a Colombo presence after two decades of discussion.
bold_phrases: Bank of Maldives establishing a Colombo presence

title: Police arrested more than 120 foreign nationals in Rajagiriya on suspicion of running coordinated financial fraud operations.
body_paragraphs: The weekend raid on multiple floors of a rented apartment complex netted suspects from China, Vietnam, Malaysia, Madagascar, the Philippines, Thailand and Cambodia, and yielded laptops and mobile phones now subject to forensic analysis, highlighting Sri Lanka’s role as a target and node in transnational cyber‑enabled crime. Several suspects escaped during the initial entry, prompting an ongoing search by Welikada Police with support from specialist units.

The arrests followed a string of cyber‑related incidents that had already unsettled investor sentiment, including an alleged $2.5 million diversion in a Treasury remittance flagged earlier in the year, and came as the Cabinet approved new data‑collection sanctions to strengthen the Central Bank’s information pipeline for policy. For a services‑driven economy that is trying to grow financial outsourcing and tourism back toward pre‑2022 levels, credible enforcement against fraud rings has become part of restoring the investment case.
bold_phrases: $2.5 million diversion in a Treasury remittance

title: IFC agreed to finance part of Sri Lanka’s first 100 MW utility‑scale solar plant in partnership with WindForce PLC.
body_paragraphs: The package included up to $18 million in local‑currency lending backed by the IDA21 Private Sector Window, alongside advisory support on engineering, grid resilience and cybersecurity to help integrate an expected 220 GWh of annual generation—roughly 1.4% of current electricity demand—at a lower levelised cost than imported thermal. It marked IFC’s first infrastructure energy deal in Sri Lanka since the crisis and the country’s first energy transaction in over a decade, meeting a gap that had left renewables largely confined to rooftop and small hydro.

The project design left room for future battery storage to stabilise variability, consistent with the Government’s 2030 target of 50–70% renewable penetration, and was expected to create more than 3,000 jobs during construction and operations. With electricity among the region’s costliest at 15,000–16,000 GWh consumption annually, shaving imported fuel use even by a percentage point improves both the balance of payments and tariff headroom.
bold_phrases: first infrastructure energy deal in Sri Lanka since the crisis

title: The Cabinet cleared Rules empowering the Central Bank to compel data submission and impose administrative sanctions for non‑compliance.
body_paragraphs: Published by Gazette on 12 March under the Central Bank of Sri Lanka Act, No. 16 of 2023, the Rules define reporting scopes, formats, confidentiality standards and penalties, and will be tabled in Parliament with the Bank’s 2025 audited financials. The move followed a bruising year in which gaps in supervisory information had complicated monetary policy and financial‑sector oversight, culminating in high‑profile fraud investigations that exposed reconciliation lapses across several institutions.

By codifying the authority to levy sanctions on tardy or inaccurate reporters, the Bank moved closer to international statistical practice, where enforcement teeth are a prerequisite for timely macroeconomic monitoring. The package also underscored the political support for strengthening technocratic functions: the President, as Finance Minister, sponsored the submission while the Governing Board met statutory deadlines for audited reporting for the first time in several years.
bold_phrases: Central Bank of Sri Lanka Act, No. 16 of 2023
reporting scopes, formats, confidentiality standards and penalties
levy sanctions on tardy or inaccurate reporters

title: Colombo inflation climbed to 5.4% year‑on‑year in April after three months near 2%.
body_paragraphs: The spike reflected a 2.99% month‑on‑month increase in the index, with non‑food items contributing 2.43 percentage points—largely transport tied to fuel costs—and food adding 0.56 points as imported energy filtered through supply chains. Core inflation also edged up to 2.8% from 2.5% in March, signalling that underlying pressures were building beyond volatile categories.

While still within the Central Bank’s 3–7% target band, the print breached the 5% midpoint that had allowed monetary policy to focus on growth since late 2025. With Brent assumptions higher due to Middle East shipping risks, authorities signalled they would allow partial pass‑through while shielding the poorest with targeted relief, a departure from the broad subsidies that had widened deficits in 2020–22.
bold_phrases: Core inflation also edged up to 2.8%

title: Prima raised the retail price of its wheat flour by Rs. 5 per kilogram, citing higher transport costs.
body_paragraphs: The adjustment took the price of a kilogram pack, including its widely used "milk" brand variant, to roughly Rs. 225 from Rs. 220, a small but broad‑based nudge given flour’s role in bakeries and household staples. With energy prices elevated and logistics strained by the Red Sea and Hormuz disruptions, the pass‑through was consistent with cost‑plus models in fast‑moving consumer goods that had shown similar step‑ups during prior oil spikes.

Although the increase was modest compared to the double‑digit jumps seen during the 2022 shortage, it landed in the same week that non‑food inflation accelerated and headline prices breached the 5% midpoint, making price‑setting psychology an immediate concern for policymakers trying to avoid a new round of indexation.
bold_phrases: raised the retail price of its wheat flour by Rs. 5 per kilogram

title: Lawmakers were told an Insolvency and Restructuring Bill could unlock over Rs. 30 billion in stalled distressed‑asset deals.
body_paragraphs: At a Committee on Public Finance hearing, investors and tax experts argued that a time‑bound, court‑supervised moratorium with creditor voting would move cases away from liquidation toward rehabilitation, reducing the drag of large exposures that banks had carried for years while SMEs faced quick parate action within ~60 days. Stakeholders warned, however, that without clean tax treatment of write‑offs and clear priority rules, new financing during restructuring would be hard to mobilise and court backlogs could blunt the Bill’s effects.

The pipeline cited—exceeding $100 million and concentrated in hotel assets—illustrated why legal certainty matters for capital recycling in a post‑crisis economy: every month of delay leaves foreign exchange idle and erodes asset values. With the Bill already gazetted, only Committee‑stage amendments remain feasible in the current session, making secondary regulations and practitioner capacity the critical determinants of impact.
bold_phrases: over Rs. 30 billion in stalled distressed‑asset deals
exceeding $100 million and concentrated in hotel assets

title: A Government committee submitted a framework to enable voting by more than 1.5 million Sri Lankans overseas.
body_paragraphs: The report to the Public Administration Ministry proposed legal changes and operational options—embassy‑based voting, postal ballots and secure online methods—for migrant workers, students, dual citizens and others abroad, together with digital registration and updated campaign‑spending rules. The exercise, completed within three months, marked the first time the State had a consolidated plan after decades of discussion that produced no implementable model.

Officials said the draft would go to Cabinet before public consultation, and also suggested exploring advance or mobile voting domestically for essential‑service workers, fishermen, the bedridden and prisoners. With remittances having reached record levels in 2025, enfranchising the diaspora would align the franchise with economic contribution and reduce the democratic deficit that has grown with outward migration.
bold_phrases: voting by more than 1.5 million Sri Lankans overseas
embassy‑based voting, postal ballots and secure online methods

title: The President chaired a high‑level review to launch electric trains on three Colombo suburban corridors from early next year.
body_paragraphs: Officials said initial operations would cover the Kelani Valley line from Maradana to Makumbura, the Main Line from Colombo Fort to Ragama, and the Coastal Line from Fort to Panadura, subject to funding, rolling‑stock delivery and power‑supply upgrades. The meeting also discussed upgrading permanent‑way standards and the possible migration from the legacy broad gauge to standard gauge where new alignments justify it.

With urban peak‑hour congestion imposing heavy productivity losses and diesel prices up due to global shocks, the shift to electric multiple units would cut operating costs and emissions versus fossil traction while raising frequency. The project forms part of the long‑delayed Colombo Suburban Railway Project, whose implementation has lagged since feasibility work was completed several planning cycles ago.
bold_phrases: Kelani Valley line from Maradana to Makumbura
migration from the legacy broad gauge to standard gauge

title: India’s High Commissioner urged a time‑bound reset on trade and connectivity, including a permanent land link across the Palk Strait.
body_paragraphs: In a keynote in Colombo, the envoy said agreements repeatedly renamed but not implemented had left value on the table despite Sri Lanka using tariff preferences on more than 65% of its exports under the FTA while India used them on only about 5%. He argued that a bridge or tunnel, grid interconnection and petroleum pipeline would change the island’s economic geography, and pointed to Indian support during Sri Lanka’s 2022 crisis and post‑Cyclone Ditwah relief of about $450 million as context for deeper integration.

Citing digital public infrastructure such as UPI and Aadhaar, he suggested Sri Lanka could share platforms to accelerate its own transformation, and noted that Indian‑linked investment accounted for roughly half of total FDI inflows in 2025 when routed through third countries. The remarks landed as Colombo weighed VAT and insolvency reforms and as regional supply chains were being weaponised in multiple theatres.
bold_phrases: permanent land link across the Palk Strait
more than 65% of its exports under the FTA
roughly half of total FDI inflows in 2025

title: Sri Lanka appointed M. Fauser as its representative to the State of Palestine with ambassador‑equivalent rank.
body_paragraphs: The Foreign Ministry said the appointee, a former Secretary of the Palestine Solidarity Committee of Sri Lanka, would advance Colombo’s long‑standing position through formal diplomatic engagement after a period in which representation lagged the rhetoric of support. The designation came amid heightened conflict in the Middle East that had already tightened global energy markets and raised shipping insurance premia, channels through which Sri Lanka has experienced indirect shocks.

While largely symbolic in trade terms—the Palestinian economy is small and constrained—the move places Sri Lanka within a cohort of countries that have opted for explicit representation at a moment when alignment choices carry reputational weight across multiple forums. The decision also followed a spate of Asian recognitions in recent years that have restored or upgraded Palestinian missions despite diplomatic headwinds.
bold_phrases: representative to the State of Palestine

title: Russia’s Health Minister began a three‑day visit for talks on pharmaceuticals, medical equipment, and specialist training.
body_paragraphs: The programme included meetings with the Health and Mass Media Minister, the Foreign Affairs Minister, and site visits to the National Hospital and the University of Colombo Faculty of Medicine to discuss academic exchanges, Ayurveda, and health tourism. The agenda signalled an attempt to diversify supply and training channels after the crisis exposed vulnerabilities in drug procurement and workforce capacity.

With the visit overlapping regional energy disruptions and a busy diplomatic calendar, Colombo sought to translate political ties into tangible sectoral cooperation that could ease costs and improve resilience in a system where medicine shortages during 2022 had undermined public confidence.
bold_phrases: pharmaceuticals, medical equipment, and specialist training
National Hospital and the University of Colombo Faculty of Medicine
